lesson.name: Schopenhauer: The World as Will - Lesson 3 — The Art of Controversy

tasks.chapterTitle: The Art of Controversy: Introduction and Theory

messages.content:
Lesson 3 — The Art of Controversy - Presentation24 Slides
Logic and Dialectic Defined
  • I prefer to define logic as the science of the laws of thought, that is, of the method of reason.
  • Dialectic I would call the art of disputation, since every conversation communicates facts or opinions and is therefore historical or deliberative.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the text, what is the proper defense against the stratagem of extension?
options: An accurate statement of the precise point at issue
questions.explanation: The text states the defense consists in an accurate statement of the point or essential question at issue.

questions.question: What does the instance or contrary example achieve?
options: It refutes a general proposition by particular cases to which it does not apply
It extends the claim to music and opera
It reduces the opponent to silence by ridicule
questions.explanation: The instance refutes the general proposition by direct reference to particular cases included in it yet to which it does not apply.

questions.question: Why does the text advise describing stratagems without regard to objective truth?
options: Because truth cannot be determined beforehand in disputation
Because all arguments are equally false
questions.explanation: The text explains that truth cannot be determined previously and disputants themselves are often uncertain.

questions.question: In the Lamarck example, what betrays that the writer is secretly conscious of being in the wrong?
options: Extension of the refutation to absurd conclusions about every part possessing human faculties
Appeal to the principle of causality
questions.explanation: The text states that by using dialectical tricks of this kind a writer betrays consciousness of being in the wrong.

questions.question: What Latin principle does the text invoke to end argument?
options: Contra negantem principia non est disputandum
questions.explanation: The text quotes: Contra negantem principia non est disputandum.

cards.front: What two procedures does direct refutation admit?
cards.back: Showing the reasons are false or admitting the reasons but showing the conclusion does not follow.

cards.front: What is the stratagem of extension?
cards.back: Carrying the opponent's proposition beyond its natural limits while narrowing one's own.

cards.front: How does the diversion produce a false conclusion?
cards.back: By joining the opponent's proposition to another acknowledged truth to yield a manifest absurdity.

cards.front: What does the instance achieve?
cards.back: It refutes a general claim by citing particular cases to which the claim does not apply.

cards.front: Why study stratagems without regard to objective truth?
cards.back: Because truth cannot be determined in advance and disputants are often uncertain.

cards.front: What must disputants agree upon before argument begins?
cards.back: Some principle by which the matter in question may be judged.

cards.front: What does reduction ad absurdum target?
cards.back: A conclusion contradicting perfectly undoubted truth or the nature of things.

cards.front: What does reduction ad hominem target?
cards.back: A conclusion contradicting the opponent's own earlier statements.

cards.front: How did Lamarck attempt to evade the polype objection?
cards.back: By extending the refutation to the absurd claim that every part of the polype would possess all human faculties.

cards.front: What is the proper defense against extension?
cards.back: An accurate statement of the precise point or essential question at issue.

slides.title: Logic and Dialectic Defined
bullets: I prefer to define logic as the science of the laws of thought, that is, of the method of reason.
Dialectic I would call the art of disputation, since every conversation communicates facts or opinions and is therefore historical or deliberative.

Logic therefore stands as the silent arbiter that exposes every inconsistency before it hardens into error, compelling the mind to retrace its steps whenever a conclusion refuses to follow from its premises.

Dialectic, by contrast, enters the living exchange where each speaker seeks not only to convey but also to prevail, so that the historical record of facts quickly yields to the deliberative struggle over which opinion shall carry the day.
slides.notes: Opens the lesson with Schopenhauer's preferred distinction drawn from the introduction.

slides.title: Ancient Usage of the Terms
bullets: By the ancients, logic and dialectic were used as synonymous terms, although thinking over and conversing are two very different things.
Plato first employed the name dialectic to mean the regular employment of reason and skill in its practice.
Consequently the ancients treated the solitary laws of thought as immediately applicable to spoken contest, so that any flaw in private reasoning could be exploited at once as a public defeat.

Plato’s restriction of the term therefore created a lasting tension: dialectic now stood above ordinary talk as the disciplined search for truth, yet it still had to operate through the very medium of conversation that invites error and deception.
slides.notes: Historical grounding from Diogenes Laertius and Plato's dialogues.

slides.title: Kant's Shift in Meaning
bullets: More recently Kant employed dialectic in a bad sense as the art of sophistical controversy.
This led many to prefer the more innocent designation of logic, yet both words originally meant the same thing.
Kant's negative usage therefore forced later philosophers into an artificial choice between two names for what had once been a single disciplined pursuit of truth through opposing reasons.
The lasting consequence was a narrowed conception of logic as mere formal correctness while the living movement of thought through contradiction was left without a respectable title.
slides.notes: Explains the modern pejorative turn before returning to synonymy.

slides.title: The Framework of All Controversy
bullets: Every controversy reduces ultimately to direct or indirect refutation of a thesis.
The direct course attacks the reasons for the thesis while the indirect attacks its results.

Thus the disputant must weigh whether to challenge the supporting grounds themselves or to trace the thesis forward until it collides with truths already granted, a choice that determines the entire direction of the exchange.

The indirect route in particular carries the risk that an opponent may accept the unwelcome consequences rather than abandon the thesis, forcing the dialectician to decide in advance how far such results must be pressed before the refutation counts as decisive.
slides.notes: Sets the skeleton that every stratagem must serve.

slides.title: Direct Refutation in Detail
bullets: Direct refutation shows the thesis is not true either by denying the major or minor premise or by denying the consequence.
We may admit the premises yet demonstrate that the stated conclusion does not follow from them.

When the major premise is denied the entire structure of the opponent argument collapses at its root, forcing a retreat to more basic claims that may themselves prove indefensible under further examination.

Denying only the consequence while granting the premises isolates the precise point at which the reasoning fails, leaving the opponent with no choice but to concede the invalid inference or to supply an entirely new logical bridge.
slides.notes: Distinguishes the two procedures within direct attack.

slides.title: Indirect Refutation Explained
bullets: Indirect refutation shows that the thesis cannot be true because its results contradict acknowledged truths.
This method proceeds by connecting the opponent's proposition with another accepted truth to yield an absurd conclusion.

The opponent is thereby compelled to choose between clinging to the original claim and surrendering some other conviction they cannot afford to lose, which turns the exchange into a test of intellectual consistency rather than a direct clash of arguments.

Because the absurdity surfaces only after the proposition is joined to a truth the audience already accepts, the refutation gains its force from that shared ground and may collapse if those background convictions are themselves withdrawn.
slides.notes: Introduces the second main course of refutation.

slides.title: The Diversion Stratagem
bullets: In the diversion we accept the opponent's proposition as true and then derive from it a manifestly false conclusion when joined to another acknowledged truth.
The conclusion contradicts either the nature of things or the opponent's own statements, forcing the original proposition to be false.
By this route the opponent is led into an untenable corner where every attempted defense only deepens the absurdity already extracted from his own premise.
The listener, witnessing the contradiction arise without any direct assault on the original claim, is quietly persuaded that the proposition was never tenable to begin with.
slides.notes: First concrete stratagem with its logical mechanism.

slides.title: Diversion Ad Rem and Ad Hominem
bullets: The false conclusion may contradict objective reality, which we call reduction ad absurdum.
It may instead contradict the opponent's earlier assertions, which we call reduction ad hominem.

When the reduction stays ad rem it compels both sides to test the claim against the shared world itself, so that any contradiction immediately reveals a flaw in the position rather than in the person holding it.

An ad hominem reduction, however, shifts scrutiny onto the opponent’s earlier statements and thereby gains a tactical victory that may leave the original question entirely untouched and therefore still open to further examination.
slides.notes: Clarifies the two targets of the diversion.

slides.title: The Instance or Contrary Example
bullets: The instance refutes a general proposition by exhibiting particular cases included in its statement yet to which it does not apply.
Such cases demonstrate that the proposition is necessarily false as formulated.
A single well-chosen instance therefore exposes the hidden overreach in any sweeping assertion, forcing its defender to hedge with exceptions that quietly surrender the claim’s original breadth.

In debate this stratagem carries a further sting: once the general rule is shown to fail on its own stated ground, every subsequent inference drawn from it loses its footing and the opponent must rebuild from narrower, less imposing premises.
slides.notes: Second basic stratagem completing the framework.

slides.title: Why Stratagems Must Be Studied
bullets: It is not easy to separate actual from apparent truth in debate, and disputants themselves are often uncertain beforehand.
Therefore I describe the tricks without regard to objective truth or falsity, since that cannot be determined in advance.
Without prior acquaintance with these stratagems the honest reasoner enters the arena unarmed and may watch the truth itself overborne by calculated confusion.
Mastery of them therefore becomes a necessary shield, for the disputant who remains ignorant grants the unscrupulous adversary an unchecked power to shape appearances at will.
slides.notes: Justifies the catalog of devices on textual grounds.

slides.title: The First Stratagem: Extension
bullets: The extension carries the opponent's proposition beyond its natural limits and gives it as wide a sense as possible.
At the same time one gives one's own proposition the narrowest limits, because the more general a statement the more objections it invites.

By stretching the claim outward in this manner one invites refutations drawn from distant cases the opponent never meant to defend, so the original point quietly slips from view while the debate rages over an inflated version of it.

The narrowed counter-claim, kept deliberately small, then functions like a compact shield that offers the fewest surfaces for counterattack and forces the opponent to waste energy striking at a target that was never truly in dispute.
slides.notes: Names and defines the first numbered stratagem.

slides.title: Defense Against Extension
bullets: The proper defense consists in an accurate statement of the precise point at issue.
One may also save the proposition by deliberately reducing it within narrower limits than first intended.

By pinning the claim to its exact boundary the defender blocks every attempt to drag the discussion onto terrain where the original assertion was never meant to stand and where refutation would therefore be effortless.

When the proposition is deliberately narrowed its safety is purchased at the cost of surrendering any larger consequence that first gave the thesis its interest, so the defender must weigh how much of the intended meaning can be abandoned without rendering the victory hollow.
slides.notes: Shows how to counter the extension.

slides.title: Example of the Extension in Drama
bullets: I asserted that the English were supreme in drama, yet my opponent extended the claim to opera and then to music.
I repelled the attack by reminding him that music is not included in dramatic art, which covers tragedy and comedy alone.

Such an unwarranted broadening turns a focused claim about dramatic excellence into an indefensible assertion across every artistic medium, compelling the defender to restore the original boundary before the entire position is lost.

The maneuver therefore exposes a deeper tension in dialectic: once the scope has been silently enlarged, victory depends less on substance than on whether the speaker can swiftly retract the discussion to its legitimate terrain.
slides.notes: Verbatim illustration from the text.

slides.title: Second Example: The Hanseatic Towns
bullets: A declared that the Peace of 1814 gave independence to all German towns of the Hanseatic League.
B cited Dantzig, which lost independence by that peace; A saved himself by restricting the claim to German towns, noting Dantzig was in Poland.
This maneuver shows how a speaker can preserve an appearance of consistency by silently trimming the scope of a universal claim once a concrete counter-instance appears.
The result is a quiet victory in form only, since the original assertion now covers far less ground than listeners were first invited to accept.
slides.notes: Further concrete case of narrowing limits.

slides.title: Lamarck's Polype and Illicit Extension
bullets: Lamarck asserted the polype has no feeling because it has no nerves, yet its behavior shows perception.
He then extended the refutation absurdly, claiming every part of the polype would possess all human faculties, thereby betraying he knew himself to be in the wrong.

Such an overreach converts a disputed biological claim into a grotesque parody that no one could defend, thereby shielding the speaker from having to revise his premises in light of the polype's evident sensitivity.

The maneuver therefore functions as an unwitting confession within the dialectic itself, alerting any attentive opponent that the original assertion has already been abandoned in favor of rhetorical excess.
slides.notes: Shows how the stratagem reveals awareness of error.

slides.title: Principle of Agreement Required
bullets: In every disputation we must agree about something and accept it as a principle by which to judge the matter.
We cannot argue with those who deny principles, for contra negantem principia non est disputandum.
Without that common ground every claim floats free of any standard that could decide it, so the speakers merely exchange assertions that never meet and the dispute grows only more stubborn with time.

The practical consequence is that one must first secure even a single shared premise before any further step can be taken, otherwise the effort to persuade collapses into two monologues that never touch.
slides.notes: States the necessary precondition of all dialectic.

slides.title: Genuine Versus Spurious Arguments
bullets: A controversy may actually proceed by the described methods or only appear to do so.
It may be supported by genuine or spurious arguments, yet the length and obstinacy of debates arise precisely because truth is hard to separate from appearance.

Consequently the victory in debate often rewards not the possession of truth but the more artful maintenance of its mere appearance, allowing error to entrench itself under the guise of reasoned triumph.

The participant therefore faces the constant demand to cultivate a double vigilance, advancing genuine grounds while at once unmasking the hidden devices that lend spurious claims their stubborn endurance.
slides.notes: Distinguishes real from apparent dialectic.

slides.title: The Role of Syllogistic Form
bullets: Direct refutation may attack the conclusion or form of the syllogism even when the premises are granted.
This shows that the stated conclusion does not logically follow from the admitted reasons.

By focusing solely on the syllogism's faulty linkage one can therefore leave the opponent's facts untouched while still stripping his conclusion of all apparent necessity, a move that often unsettles listeners more than any quarrel over the premises themselves.

The resulting exposure of invalid inference compels the adversary either to abandon his claim or to supply an entirely new chain of reasoning, thereby converting an apparent concession into a decisive tactical retreat.
slides.notes: Focuses on formal attack within direct refutation.

slides.title: Why Debates Become Obstinate
bullets: Debates grow long and obstinate because it is difficult to make out the truth regarding the opponent's actual position.
Even the disputants are not certain about objective truth beforehand, so stratagems proliferate.
Because neither party can fix the other's meaning with certainty, every reply risks answering a phantom claim, so the discussion drifts into endless qualification and counter-qualification.

The result is that victory comes to depend less on the strength of reasons than on the skill with which each speaker keeps his own position elastic while pinning the opponent to an ever-narrower formulation.
slides.notes: Psychological observation on controversy.

slides.title: Reducing the Proposition to Its Essence
bullets: When the opponent extends our claim we must restore it to its essential question at issue.
Accurate restatement prevents the proposition from being made to cover more than it was intended to assert.

If we permit the claim to swell beyond its original boundaries the opponent gains an open field in which to plant objections that have nothing to do with our actual assertion, and the contest drifts into a fog of irrelevant quarrels.

Only by repeatedly drawing the discussion back to the precise nucleus can we keep the adversary from turning our own words into a shield for doctrines we never endorsed, thereby protecting the force of whatever evidence we truly possess.
slides.notes: Reinforces the defensive move against extension.

slides.title: The Instance as Direct Reference
bullets: The instance works by direct reference to particular cases included in the general proposition yet excluded by its actual application.
This shows the proposition as stated cannot hold universally.
Aristotle already noted this device in the Topica.

Thus the opponent is compelled to watch his universal rule founder on a single concrete case that belongs within its stated scope yet escapes its intended reach, compelling either a humiliating retreat or an awkward narrowing of the original claim.
slides.notes: Deepens the account of the contrary example.

slides.title: Stratagems Serve Both Sides
bullets: The same devices of extension, diversion, and instance may be used to defend a true thesis or to attack a false one.
Their study therefore belongs to the art of dialectic regardless of the objective truth of the matter under discussion.
Thus a dialectician armed with these stratagems can rescue a sound claim from clever attack or, with equal facility, keep an absurdity afloat until the opponent tires or loses the thread.

The resulting tension is that mastery of dialectic sharpens the mind for both defense of truth and concealment of error, demanding an independent resolve to aim the tools toward genuine insight rather than mere victory.
slides.notes: Concludes the treatment of stratagems.
